") [Versions Notes](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916/notes) Abstract -------- With the continuous improvement of global technological levels and the increasing demand for high-performance alloy materials in national economic construction, the traditional single principal component alloy is increasingly unable to meet people’s increasing service needs. High-entropy alloys play an important role in aerospace, mechanical manufacturing, biomedicine, energy development and other engineering fields because of their unique physical, chemical and mechanical properties. Based on the concept of high-entropy alloys, the high-entropy effect, lattice distortion effect, sluggish diffusion effect and cocktail effect of high-entropy alloys are represented in this paper. The common preparation methods of high-entropy alloys are summarized according to the classification of melting-casting method, mechanical alloying method and coating method. The strength and toughness, wear resistance, corrosion resistance, high temperature resistance, fatigue resistance, radiation resistance and magnetic properties of the high-entropy alloys are discussed. The application prospect of high-entropy alloys is summarized, and the future research and development direction of high-entropy alloys are prospected on this basis. Keywords: [high-entropy alloys](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=high-entropy+alloys); [preparation methods](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=preparation+methods); [wear resistance](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=wear+resistance); [corrosion resistance](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=corrosion+resistance); [application prospect](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=application+prospect) 1. Introduction --------------- As one of the three pillars of modern civilization, materials play an irreplaceable role in the process of continuous development of society. In the course of the advancement and evolution of human history from the Stone Age to the Bronze Age and then to the Iron Age, every major social change was accompanied by the emergence of new materials. The first technological revolution is based on the development of materials such as steel and copper. The second technological revolution is based on the development of alloy steel, aluminum alloy and various non-metallic materials. The third technological revolution has realized large-scale industrialization and civil production of synthetic materials and semiconductor materials. Materials are the basis of modern technology and the core of all scientific and technological progress. The development of materials has not only changed the way of human life, but has also promoted the progress of productivity and the innovation of science and technology. Composite materials, amorphous materials and high-entropy alloys are the three hotspots in the field of materials in the past 20 years. In a socio-economically developed environment with the theme of informatization, intelligent accelerated development, energy saving and emission reduction, improving the comprehensive performance of alloys is the overall goal of new material research and development. As a new type of alloy with high hardness, high heat resistance, strong corrosion resistance, high wear resistance and fatigue resistance, high-entropy alloys are different from any existing traditional alloy. As a new alloy system, high-entropy alloys have important research value and broad application prospects. Therefore, the design, preparation and application of high-entropy alloys have become an important direction for the development of new materials [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B1-coatings-13-01916),[2](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B2-coatings-13-01916)]. 2. Concept of High-Entropy Alloys --------------------------------- #### 2.1. Proposal of High-Entropy Alloys As early as the end of the 18th century, Franz Karl Achard carried out experimental research on multicomponent alloys of five to seven elements. The innovative work was not discovered and reported until 1963 by Professor Cyril Stanley Smith, which is the earliest recorded study on high-entropy alloys. In 1993, Professor Cantor of Cambridge University first expounded the concept of multiprincipal alloys [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B3-coatings-13-01916)]. In the subsequent research work, Cantor [[4](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B4-coatings-13-01916)] also pointed out that the traditional alloy design concept caused the lack of research on the alloy phase diagram. Under the traditional alloy design concept, the research on the endpoint or edge region of the phase diagram has been greatly developed, while the large area in the middle of the phase diagram is poorly understood, especially the phase diagram analysis of the alloy in the presence of equal moles of several or more elements. As shown in [Figure 1](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#fig_body_display_coatings-13-01916-f001), there is a large degree of blank area in the phase diagram of binary and ternary alloys. Therefore, it is necessary to expand and supplement the alloy phase diagram. It is generally believed that there are two ways to achieve this goal. One is to increase the alloy element content on the basis of traditional alloy design, and the other is to use an equal atom substitution method, that is, to replace individual atoms in the component with other elements. Through the above two methods, a multicomponent alloy system with closer composition can be obtained. ![Image 7: Coatings 13 01916 g001](https://www.mdpi.com/coatings/coatings-13-01916/article_deploy/html/images/coatings-13-01916-g001-550.jpg)[](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#fig_body_display_coatings-13-01916-f001) **Figure 1.** Schematic diagram of ternary and quaternary alloy systems [[5](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B5-coatings-13-01916)]. Around 1995, scholars represented by Professor Yeh have also carried out a lot of basic research on multiprincipal alloy [[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B6-coatings-13-01916),[7](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B7-coatings-13-01916)]. In 2004, Professor Yeh proposed the concept of the “high-entropy alloy” for the first time, which was expressed as: an alloy composed of five or more elements mixed in a near-equal molar ratio, with each element content (atomic ratio) greater than 5% and less than 35% [[7](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B7-coatings-13-01916)]. On this basis, the research of multiprincipal alloys has been raised to a new height. In 2016, Lim from Singapore mentioned in an article published in Nature journal that the advent of the “high-entropy alloy” has created unprecedented broad prospects for the innovative development of materials metallurgy science, and provided new ideas and opportunities for the research and development of special property materials that can be used under extreme conditions and the upgrading and replacement of tradi -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --- SOURCE 2: A comprehensive review on advances in high entropy alloys --- URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1526612523011441 SUMMARY: A comprehensive review on advances in high entropy alloys: Fabrication and surface modification methods, properties, applications, and future prospects - ScienceDirect ### A novel Al0.5CrCuNiV 3d transition metal high-entropy alloy: phase analysis, microstructure and compressive properties ### Effects of Al addition on the microstructure and mechanical property of Al xCoCrFeNi high-entropy alloys 2024, Surface and Coatings Technology Show abstract High-entropy alloys (HEAs) exhibit significant potential for advanced wear and corrosion-resistant applications. High Entropy Alloys (HEAs) are a developing class of alloys displaying unique and advanced mechanical, tribological, thermal stability, and corrosion properties. Show abstract A comprehensive review of Additive Manufacturing (AM) of High Entropy Alloys (HEAs) is presented, exploring the opportunities for producing advanced components with tailored properties through design-based alloys. comprehensive review on advances in high entropy alloys: Fabrication and surface modification methods, properties, applications, and future prospects =============================================================================================================================================================== Author links open overlay panel S. Aravind Krishna a, Niveditha Noble a, N.Radhika a, Bassiouny Saleh b c Show more Add to Mendeley Share Cite [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmapro.2023.12.039](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmapro.2023.12.039 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S1526612523011441&orderBeanReset=true) Abstract -------- Scientific and technological improvements have occurred over time, including alloys that have developed from normal to intricate compositions, consequently enhancing performance and characteristics and promoting advancements. [High Entropy Alloy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/high-entropy-alloys "Learn more about High Entropy Alloy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HEA) is an innovative state-of-the-art [material class](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/materials-class "Learn more about material class from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with five more elemental constituents than conventional alloys, which typically have one or two base elements. HEAs have uncommon microstructures and numerous appealing properties, such as great [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), great hardness, rich resistance to corrosion, fracture and [fatigue resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fatigue-of-materials "Learn more about fatigue resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), high-temperature stability, and so on, that outperform conventional alloys. These superior properties of enriched HEAs are extremely interesting not only for research purposes but also for a variety of promising potential applications. Recent research has focused on the use of HEAs for coating and surface modification. [Surface coatings](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/surface-coating "Learn more about Surface coatings from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") containing HEA have been shown to improve surface performance and mechanical properties. As a result, this paper discusses a detailed review of HEA processing methods, state-of-the-art HEA-based coating and joining processes, and their mechanical properties. Moreover, this critical review comprehensively describes the different applications and future research directions required for designing these alloys. The predicted results of this article can be deemed crucial for future manufacturing and analysis studies for HEAs, and they are helpful for researchers and producers in this field. Introduction ------------ Material science advancements have led to the development of advanced engineering materials and alloys that support highly demanding applications. Since the beginning of the industrial revolution, metals and alloys have conspicuously figured in all major aspects increasingly adopting newly developed materials. Abounding research has been accomplished in the development of novel and advanced materials. One such novel class of material that is recently being exploited more and more is the high entropy alloys (HEAs) [1]. The impetus for this novel material stemmed from the need to expand the limits of knowledge concerning alloys with more than one primary element. Since it was proposed in 2004, this new concept has been heeded, and various studies have been reported over the years, as shown in Fig. 1a, and the global contribution in the HEA research field is shown in Fig. 1b [2,3]. Almost all conventional alloys, including aluminium, ferrous, zinc, titanium, magnesium, etc., and its alloys, are based on a single element. HEA is made by combining five or more elements with atomic compositions ranging from 5% to 35% [4]. As indicated by current phase diagrams and physical metallurgy, such multicomponent alloys may produce intermetallic compounds and many phases, resulting in brittle and complex microstructures with likely limited practical values. Experiment results show that higher mixing entropy of these alloys improves random simple structure solid solution phase formation. In contrast to intermetallic compounds and other complex phases with complicated chemical compositions, HEA crystalline structures are made of simple solid solution phases such as Body Centred Cubic (BCC), Face Centred Cubic (FCC), Hexagonal Close Packing (HCP), or a combination of these phases [5]. Fig. 2 depicts the four main core effects of HEAs. The research related to HEAs is continually advancing over to the conventional alloys, and that is supported by the properties like higher strength and hardness, improved ductility and fatigue, elevated temperature stability, wear and corrosive resistance [[6], [7], [8], [9]]. Since each HEA with minor elemental modifications is a new alloy base, there are virtually limitless combinations possible [10]. The alloy design procedure has seen tremendous success, and significant efforts have been made to develop and apply many HEAs in various fields due to their superior performance. Regardless, the current research and understanding of HEAs appears to be the tip of the iceberg, with many uncharted insights on HEAs being revealed on a regular basis. Because of their curious and exceptional properties and assuring research aspects, numerous new revelations in HEAs are arising past the current books and review papers. As to durability and material quality, mechanical properties are the foremost decisive aspect where phases assume a critical part as FCC-structured HEA displays lower yield strength and great plasticity whereas BCC-structured HEA presents higher yield strength and lower plasticity. The HEAs developed thus far proclaimed to exhibit unrivalled physical, functional and mechanical properties surpassing that of most alloys and pure metals. The tremendous scope of compositions altogether builds the number of alloys to characterise. Changing only one component in the same alloy class can impact on the microstructure and properties significantly as the elements generally are more focused in HEAs [11]. The properties and microstructures are affected by the fabrication method and post-processing method utilized. The mechanical properties of HEA coatings against wear and hardness have been confirmed in experimental studies; thus, the coatings have extraordinary possibilities, as well as future trends and challenges. The process of HEA synthesis and their associated coatings and joining processes has extraordinary potential in addressing the serious interest in elite execution as surface coating serving in extreme weather conditions. The demand for coating and joining in highly demanding applications grows by the day in order to further improve the properties' ability to perform in extreme conditions. Concerning the potential benefits and applications of HEAs, this review summarizes the evolution and state of the art of these alloys as well as the critical factors linked to their properties. In light of this, the successive sections of this paper review the historical background of HEAs, various classifications of HEA alloy families and their microstructure which impacts the final properties, followed by the deformation behaviour of HEAs, including plastic and elastic behaviours and their properties, powder processing methods such as mechanical alloying, atomisation, and isostatic pressing. Furthermore, the current difficulties and future perspectives are deliberately highlighted and addressed. Where humans dealt with raw materials from the beginning until most of their needs were met [10]. However, as human life progressed, it required materials with specific and contradictory properties at the same time, which nature unfortunately does not provide directly. So, after a while, humans came up with the first solution to this problem via combining (in molten state) one metal with other metals or Basic deformation behaviour in high entropy alloys -------------------------------------------------- HEA and its deformation behaviour is a subject of active research. Considering their high configurational entropy and complex microstructures, the deformation behaviour of HEAs can differ fundamentally from that of conventional alloys. One of the inimitable characteristics of HEAs is their high protection from plastic deformation. HEAs typically exhibit high strength and low ductility, which can be credited to their high mixing entropy and complex microstructures. However, some HEAs have also Fabrication methods of high entropy alloy powders ------------------------------------------------- HEA coatings based on powder are broadly utilized because of their ease of production and capacity to produce thick coating. Powder based coatings offer a few benefits over other forms such as offering phenomenal control over the composition and microstructure that can be adapted for explicit applications and ability to produce thick coating providing resistance against wear and corrosion. In addition, powder based coating techniques offer adaptability regarding coating thickness, coating Introduction and historical background of HEA coatings ------------------------------------------------------ Because of the HEA principles and their outstanding functional and mechanical qualities, HEA-based coatings are being researched and developed at a rapid speed. The origin of HEA coatings traces back to the early 2000s. The theoretical concept of multicomponent and HEAs emerged in the 1980s and was formally introduced in 2004 by Professor Ye Junwei. Substantial research attention from diverse countries ensued after 2004 when the inaugural HEA were engineered to withstand exceptionally elevated Potential applications ---------------------- The demand for structural and functional materials is increasing in a few areas as science and engineering continue to advance and progress in many areas over existing superalloys. Because of their unrivalled functional, mechanical, and design elemental compositions, HEAs-based coating and joining are expected to find assuring applications in a variety of fields, including the engineering and industrial sectors, as shown in Fig. 25. Variations in process parameters and fabrication techniques, in addition to the composition of HEA elements, influence the microstructural and surface performance of the substrate, including phase constitution. George _et al._ ### High-entropy alloys ### Nat Rev Mater (2019) View more references Cited by (88) ------------- * ### [High entropy alloys: A review of preparation techniques, properties and industry applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0925838824042798) 2025, Journal of Alloys and Compounds Show abstract High entropy alloys (HEAs) represent a novel class of multi-component materials characterized by a paradigm-shifting design that incorporates five or more principal elements in nearly equal proportions. This configuration results in high configurational entropy and the formation of solid solutions. This review succinctly outlines the theoretical foundations of HEAs, including the entropy of the alloy and its thermodynamic stability, following a discussion of design principles and first-principles calculations that are crucial for alloy optimization, we provide a comprehensive review of various preparation techniques, such as mechanical alloying, magnetron sputtering, vacuum smelting, and additive manufacturing, emphasizing their influence on the properties of HEAs. The paper meticulously examines the core effects of these alloys—high entropy, lattice distortion, slow diffusion, and cocktail effects—which contribute to their unique attributes. Additionally, the review explores the promising applications of HEAs in sectors such as aerospace, energy, chemical industries, hydrogen storage, and ocean engineering, highlighting the increasing demand for advanced materials. Looking ahead, we propose future research directions that focus on the interplay between multi-scale structures and properties, interdisciplinary preparation technologies, and sustainable alloy recovery and reuse strategies. This review aims to provide both theoretical insights and practical guidelines for the rapidly evolving field of high entropy alloys. * ### [Laser clad CoCrFeNiTixNby hypoeutectic high-entropy alloy coating: Effects of Ti and Nb content on mechanical, wear and corrosion properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0257897224009770) 2024, Surface and Coatings Technology Show abstract High-entropy alloys (HEAs) exhibit significant potential for advanced wear and corrosion-resistant applications. This study investigates the influence of Ti and Nb doping on the microstructure, phase composition, and properties of CoCrFeNiTi x Nb y HEAs synthesized using high-speed laser cladding (HLC). The results demonstrate that increasing Ti and Nb content transforms the coating structure from a face-centered cubic (FCC) phase to a hybrid structure comprising FCC, body-centered cubic (BCC), and Laves phases, leading to a linear enhancement in surface hardness. The incorporation of Ti and Nb not only promotes the preferred orientation of the FCC phase (111) crystal plane but also significantly enhances tensile strength, though this comes at the expense of reduced plasticity. Specifically, the CoCrFeNiTi 0.6 Nb 0.15 coating attains an ideal balance between strength and ductility, with a tensile strength of 1641.8 MPa and a tensile strain of 15.9%, achieved through the formation of a eutectic structure. This coating also exhibits superior wear resistance and outstanding corrosion resistance, which are attributed to the stability of the passivation film, reinforced by the (111) crystal plane and high-density dislocations. These findings provide both theoretical and empirical foundations for the design of high-performance HEAs, underscoring their potential in industrial applications requiring robust wear and corrosion resistance. * ### [Recent advancements in lightweight high entropy alloys – A comprehensive review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2588840424000520) 2024, International Journal of Lightweight Materials and Manufacture Show abstract Current advancements in technology enables the enhancement and refinement of alloys to address the demands of expanding industrial applications. High Entropy Alloys (HEAs) are a developing class of alloys displaying unique and advanced mechanical, tribological, thermal stability, and corrosion properties. HEAs have unpredictable structures and compositions displaying enhanced performance and characteristics. Unique microstructures can be achieved through multi-principal elements and HEAs usually outperform conventionally made alloys. Lightweight HEAs (LWHEAs) are a category of HEAs with alloy density less than 6 g/cm 3 and are potentially applicable in the automobile and aerospace industries. The superior characteristics make LWHEAs an extremely interesting space for research. Recent research has focused on effective manufacturing methods for processing alloys, coatings, and surface modifications. The current work discusses a comprehensive review of fabrication processes, mechanical, tribological, and corrosion behavior of LWHEAs. The review also highlights the future scope of research and directions for designing LWHEAs. enriched_points.expanded_content: High-entropy alloys (HEAs) are transforming various industries, including aerospace, biomedicine, and energy, due to their superior mechanical and thermal properties, enhancing performance in demanding environments.

Continued exploration of HEAs is essential to unveil their full potential, optimize compositions, and develop innovative applications, addressing gaps in understanding and material behavior under extreme conditions.

HEAs could set new benchmarks in material science, driving advancements in manufacturing processes, sustainability, and performance metrics, particularly in sectors requiring high durability and resistance.
